name: Canyon to N of road 1 mi. S of Apache Nat. Forest
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site in the Greenlee County high desert near the Apache National Forest. Collect agate, chalcedony, and quartz specimens with easy access from the Coronado Trail.
description:
This BLM rockhounding site sits at 5,302 feet elevation in the transition zone between the Chiricahua Mountains and the Apache-Sitgreaves National Forests in eastern Arizona. The canyon setting offers excellent exposure of Tertiary-age volcanic and rhyolitic formations that host fine agate nodules, banded chalcedony, and colorful jasper specimens. The terrain features gentle slopes and accessible washes where weathered specimens concentrate after seasonal runoff.

The location benefits from proximity to the Coronado Trail (US 191), one of Arizona's most scenic drives, making it easily accessible for day trips from nearby towns. The high desert climate and volcanic geology typical of this region create ideal conditions for agate formation. Specimens here typically range from thumb-sized to fist-sized, with some larger chunks available in productive washes.

As public BLM land, this site welcomes recreational rockhounds year-round under standard personal-use collection rules. The gentle slope and well-defined canyon provide good visibility for spotting specimens. Most productive collecting occurs in and around wash beds where water action has naturally sorted and exposed specimens over millennia.
history: This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary-age volcanic activity and rhyolitic flows deposited extensive silica-rich minerals. The Gila River formation and related volcanism created the conditions for widespread agate and chalcedony nodule formation. Prospectors and rockhounds have worked the washes and outcrops throughout the 20th century, making this one of Arizona's accessible high-desert rockhounding destinations.
tips: Search the main wash bed and tributary drainages where water erosion concentrates specimens - look for colorful agate with banding patterns and chalcedony chunks in cream, white, and tan colors. Use a digging bar or small pick to dislodge specimens from loose soil and weathered volcanic matrix, especially where washes undercut banks. Early morning light makes spotting translucent agates easier; scan slopes above the wash for specimens weathering out of rock faces. Stay hydrated at this elevation and watch for flash flood potential during monsoon season - do not enter if rain threatens.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5,302 feet with gentle to moderate slopes. Desert scrub vegetation including creosote, yucca, and scattered ponderosa pine at higher elevations. Walking distance from pullout to productive areas is typically 0.25 to 0.5 miles. Rocky footing with loose volcanic material requires sturdy boots. Canyon washes provide easier walking than surrounding slopes. Soil is shallow and rocky with many exposed rock faces and outcrops.
best_season: October - April. The high desert elevation and afternoon monsoon risk make summer uncomfortable and dangerous. Fall and spring offer mild temperatures, lower water hazards, and excellent specimen visibility. Winter can bring occasional snow at this elevation but usually clears quickly. Avoid June through September due to heat and flash flood danger in canyon washes.
written_directions: From Morenci, take US 191 south (the Coronado Trail) for approximately 12 miles. Watch for the canyon entrance on the west side of the highway approximately 1 mile south of the Apache National Forest boundary marker. A small dirt pullout is available on the east shoulder of US 191. Park here and hike west into the canyon system. The main wash is visible from the road and accessible within a 5-10 minute walk.
road_conditions: Paved highway (US 191). Access pullout may be rough dirt or gravel; high-clearance not required for parking area.
parking_notes: Dirt pullout on east shoulder of US 191 with space for 2-3 vehicles. No facilities. Unimproved but accessible year-round.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Specimens must be removed by hand tools only. Pack out all trash. Do not disturb archaeological sites or vegetation beyond specimen collection. Respect private property boundaries if adjacent.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Pack out all trash
No disturbance of archaeological sites
nearest_services: Morenci, approximately 12 miles north, has gas, food, and basic supplies. Clifton, approximately 20 miles northwest, offers more extensive services including restaurants, lodging, and equipment shops.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Gentle slopes and wash bottoms provide easy walking. Rocky footing requires sturdy boots but no scrambling necessary. Uneven volcanic terrain means wheelchair access is not practical. Parking pullout is ground-level. Most productive areas within 0.5 miles of parking.

faq.question: What types of agates and gemstones can I find at this canyon location north of Apache National Forest?
faq.answer: This BLM site produces fine agate with banding, chalcedony in cream and tan colors, and colorful jasper. Most specimens range from thumb-sized to fist-sized. The volcanic geology of the area creates ideal conditions for silica-based gemstones. Specimens are typically found loose in washes or weathering from volcanic outcrops.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at this Greenlee County site free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding is completely free on this BLM land with no permit required. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use under standard FLPMA regulations. Just park at the pullout on US 191 and start collecting.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Morenci on the Coronado Trail?
faq.answer: October through April offers the best conditions. Temperatures are mild, flash flood risk is low, and specimen visibility is excellent. Avoid June through September due to afternoon monsoons, heat, and flash flood danger in canyon washes. Winter occasionally brings snow but is still accessible.

faq.question: How difficult is collecting at this canyon site for beginners and families?
faq.answer: This site is beginner and family-friendly with easy terrain and no technical skills required. The gentle slopes and accessible washes make it suitable for children ages 6 and up. Specimens are visible and collectible with basic hand tools like a digging bar or small pick.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to rockhound this BLM location successfully?
faq.answer: Bring a small digging bar or pick, a 5-gallon bucket for specimens, sturdy hiking boots, and plenty of water. A hand lens helps identify chalcedony banding patterns. Sunscreen, a hat, and a headlamp are useful. Wear gloves to protect hands from sharp volcanic material and snakes.

faq.question: How do I get to this rockhounding site from Morenci and what should I watch for driving?
faq.answer: From Morenci, take US 191 (Coronado Trail) south for about 12 miles. Look for the canyon entrance on the west side of the highway approximately 1 mile south of the Apache National Forest boundary. A small dirt pullout on the east shoulder provides parking. The main wash is visible from the road and accessible via a short hike.

meta_title: Agate & Chalcedony at Apache Forest Canyon, Arizona
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Morenci. Collect agate, chalcedony, and jasper in scenic canyon. Easy access from US 191. No permit required.
